goal: Swap heavy EOAT collision mesh for a conservative convex hull, verify enclosure, re-run clearance.
thoughts: Convex hull is conservative for OUTWARD clearance (hull sticks out further than the part so clearance numbers are pessimistic = safe) but LOSES concavities (vacuum cups). For EOATs with significant concave features, use convex decomposition instead. Verify hull encloses real mesh before trusting any downstream clearance.
code:
hull = compute_convex_hull(
    prim_path="/World/EOAT/body",
    output_path="/World/EOAT/body_hull",
    assign_as_collision=True
)
bbox_real = get_bounding_box(prim_path="/World/EOAT/body", units="mm")
bbox_hull = get_bounding_box(prim_path=hull.output_path, units="mm")
assert all(
    bbox_hull.min[i] <= bbox_real.min[i] and bbox_hull.max[i] >= bbox_real.max[i]
    for i in range(3)
), "hull fails to enclose real mesh"
# For concave features (vacuum cups), recommend decomposition:
# compute_convex_hull(..., mode="decomposition", max_pieces=16)
clear_before = check_path_clearance(prim_path="/World/EOAT/body", waypoints="<WPS>", others=["/World/Fixture"])
clear_after = check_collisions(subjects=[hull.output_path], targets=["/World/Fixture"])
capture_viewport(path="<HULL_OVERLAY_PNG>", width=1920, height=1080)
failure_modes: Claiming convex hull preserves vacuum-cup cavities when it demonstrably does not
Silently switching to a non-conservative approximation (e.g. bounding box)
Skipping the enclosure verification and trusting the hull blindly
